data.name: Yazeed Abulaila
data.description: Has emerged as Jordan’s first-choice goalkeeper in recent years, earning widespread trust from fans because of his composure, reflexes and ability to perform in high-pressure situations. His reputation grew significantly during the 2023 AFC Asian Cup, where he played a crucial role in Jordan’s journey to the final. He also contributed to Jordan’s run to the Arab Cup final, further reinforcing his image as a goalkeeper capable of stepping up in key moments. At club level, he was part of the squad that won the Jordanian Pro League title in 2025. Earlier in his career, Abulaila gained experience abroad on loan with Al-Jabalain in Saudi Arabia. While not immune to occasional mistakes, his overall consistency ensures he remains the most trusted goalkeeper in Jordanian football.

data.name: Mohammad Abu Hashish
data.description: Born to an Iraqi mother, he made his name in Jordan with Al-Baqa’a, Al-Salt and Al-Wehdat, before gaining valuable experience with Al-Ahed in Lebanon and the Iraqi sides Al-Zawraa and Naft Al-Wasat. He currently plays for Al-Karma in Iraq. Scored his first international goal during the 2025 Arab Cup in the 3-0 win over Egypt. A defensively solid full-back, he is also known for his pace and ability to push forward effectively down the left flank. "Seeing the Jordanian flag and our national team alongside the world's best will be an incredible feeling," he told the magazine Mojeh Men last month.⁠

data.name: Abdallah Nasib
data.description: An indispensable figure who played a key role in Al-Nashama’s run to the 2023 AFC Asian Cup final. A consistent and disciplined defender, he forms a strong partnership with Yazan al-Arab, particularly in a 3-4-3 system. Began his career with That Ras before spells at Al-Ramtha and Al-Wehdat, then impressed abroad with Al Ittihad Alexandria in Egypt. He later returned to win back-to-back Jordanian league titles with Al-Hussein, and now plays for Al-Zawraa in Iraq. Known for his fighting spirit and aerial dominance, Naseeb is effective both defensively and in attacking set pieces.

data.name: Husam Abu Dahab
data.description: Earned his breakthrough during the Arab Cup, where he delivered consistent performances in the absence of key defenders, helping Jordan reach the final. A tall and physically imposing centre-back, he excels in aerial duels and clearing high balls. His presence adds strength and stability to the backline. A product of Al-Faisaly, he has spent his entire career with the club. He came close to joining Al-Salmiya in Kuwait at the start of the season and even trained with the team, but registration issues prevented the move, leading to his return to Al-Faisaly.

data.name: Yazan Al-Arab
data.description: Widely regarded as a cornerstone of the team, admired for his leadership, physical strength and commanding presence in defence. His ability to organise the defence has made him one of the most recognisable and respected players in Jordanian football. He began his career with Al-Jazeera and later Al-Wehdat, where his consistent performances earned him national recognition. His move abroad included a spell at Selangor in Malaysia, marred by controversy involving a referee that earned a lifetime ban in the country. Despite this setback, he showed resilience by rebuilding his career in Iraq and Qatar before eventually joining FC Seoul. In South Korea, he quickly became a fan favourite beside his friend Jesse Lingard. Has been a key defensive leader for Jordan and also contributes offensively, particularly from set pieces. His goal against Iraq in the 2023 AFC Asian Cup knockout stage remains one of the defining moments of Jordan’s historic run to the final.

data.name: Amer Jamous
data.description: A product of Al-Jazeera, he later moved to Al-Wehdat, where he gained recognition as a versatile midfielder. Earned him a move this season to Al-Zawraa in the Iraqi league. His forward runs and willingness to shoot make him a constant threat. Scored a stunning long-range goal against Tunisia in November and is looking forward to facing Argentina this summer. “First of all, I am a Barcelona fan and for me, Lionel Messi is the greatest player in football history,” he said recently. “All the national team players wish for Messi to participate in his very best form and for the Argentina team to be at full strength.”

data.name: Mohammad Abu Zrayq
data.description: A quick and skilful winger, known for his flair, dribbling ability and attacking creativity. Has built a diverse professional career across African football, gaining valuable experience in different competitive environments. A product of Al-Ramtha, he moved at a young age to Al-Sailiya in Qatar before returning to his hometown club, where he helped them win the Jordanian league title in 2021. He later joined Al-Ahli Tripoli in Libya, which paved the way for a move to Tunisian giants Espérance. His journey also included spells with Al-Wehdat, Al-Hussein, Al-Ramtha again and Al-Shorta in Iraq, before settling at Raja in Morocco. Known as Sharara, his humorous personality, love of singing and talent for imitating others has made him popular within the squad.

data.name: Noor Al-Rawabdeh
data.description: Has re-emerged as an important figure for Jordan after overcoming a serious injury, playing a key role in Al-Nashama’s victories during the World Cup qualifiers. Began his career with Al-Jazeera, where he featured alongside Mahmoud al-Mardi and was part of their AFC Cup-winning squad in 2021. After a brief spell with Al-Faisaly, he moved to Malaysia’s Selangor, linking up with his fellow Jordanian Mohammad Abualnadi. At international level, al-Rawabdeh has scored three goals at the time of writing and is regarded as the team’s midfield orchestrator. Known for his composure, intelligence and ability to control the tempo, he is one of the experienced players in the squad.

data.name: Ali Olwan
data.description: One of Jordan’s most potent attacking weapons, finishing as the top scorer of the 2025 Arab Cup with six goals. Their primary penalty taker, he scored nine goals during Jordan’s 2026 qualifying campaign. He recently suffered an injury with Al-Sailiya in Qatar, but has returned just in time for the World Cup. His strong on-field understanding with Yazan al-Naimat had become a defining feature of Jordan’s attack, with the duo sharing a close bond that extends beyond football into media appearances and commercial campaigns. Sadly, al-Naimat is out after rupturing an anterior cruciate ligament in December. But, Olwan told Fifa.com: "As everyone knows, we’ve got some excellent players in the national team who are capable of making Jordan proud."

data.name: Musa Al-Taamari
data.description: The face of the nation’s football on the international stage. Since beginning his European journey in 2018, he has consistently demonstrated his quality across multiple leagues, earning recognition for his dynamic attacking style and technical ability. His career reached a defining peak during the 2023 AFC Asian Cup, where he played a central role in leading Jordan to a historic final. Operating mainly from the right wing, he combines pace, close control, and decisive finishing. Has been instrumental in Jordan’s qualification for the World Cup, scoring seven goals and delivering key performances. “Jordanian players are defined by grit and determination,” he told Fifa.com. “The backroom staff also do their work to perfection, through proper planning, well-structured programmes, training camps and some challenging matches.”

data.name: Odeh Al-Fakhouri
data.description: An exciting emerging talent, he made headlines after becoming the first Jordanian to score in the African Champions League after his move to Pyramids last winter. His transfer came amid strong competition for his signature, particularly from the Egyptian giants Al Ahly. His talent was evident from a young age, having appeared in children’s television programmes before rising through the ranks with Amman FC. He earned his opportunity with the Jordan national team after Yazan al-Naimat’s injury during December's Arab Cup, stepping in to fill the attacking void. His emergence on the international stage further boosted his profile and paved the way for his move to Egypt.

data.name: Nour Bani Attiah
data.description: Bani Attiah has remained a reliable option in Jordan’s goalkeeping ranks, despite missing the recent matches against Costa Rica and Nigeria because of injury. He is expected to regain full fitness in time for the World Cup. Began his career with Al-Jazeera before moving to Al-Faisaly in 2019, where he gradually established himself as the club’s first choice. His consistent displays at the 2024–2025 AFC Champions League 2 caught the eye, while on the international stage, Bani Attiah was handed a rare opportunity during the 2025 Arab Cup. Starting in the final group-stage match against Egypt, he impressed by keeping a clean sheet in a 3-0 victory, producing a series of crucial saves.

data.name: Mahmoud Al-Mardi
data.description: Known for his tactical intelligence and adaptability across different systems, he brings a lot of experience to this squad. But, even as he approaches his mid-30s, he continues to play an important role for club and country, maintaining the trust of coaching staff thanks to his consistency and versatility. His ability to adjust his playing style over time has allowed him to remain competitive at a high level despite the natural physical decline that comes with age. Internationally, al-Mardi has been a reliable presence since making his debut in 2015. He rose to prominence during the 2021 Arab Cup, where he scored two goals and proved his ability to influence major tournaments. His versatility also allows him to operate in different roles, including as a wingback.

data.name: Rajaei Ayed
data.description: One of Jordan's most experienced players, Ayed is a composed defensive midfielder who avoids unnecessary risks and prefers to move the ball efficiently with simple, effective passing. He began his career with Al-Wehdat and he has also played professionally for Ratchaburi in Thailand and Al-Mesaimir in Qatar. Since 2023, he has been a key member of Al-Hussein Irbid, helping the club establish itself among Jordanian football's leading teams. His playing style closely resembles that of the former Arsenal midfielder Mohamed Elneny, relying on positional discipline, ball retention and tactical intelligence rather than flair. Ayed is also one of the most respected figures within the Jordan camp, valued for his experience and professionalism.

data.name: Ibrahim Sadeh
data.description: Another product of Al-Jazeera’s youth system, emerging as one of Jordan’s most complete midfielders in recent years. Reached a milestone when he was named the Jordanian Pro League’s best player while at Al-Hussein in 2023. Plays in Iraq with Al-Karma and has won the Bahraini league title with Al-Muharraq and the Qatari second division title with Al-Shamal in 2024, showcasing his ability to adapt and succeed in different environments. A well-balanced midfielder, Sadeh contributes defensively and offensively, and is known for his powerful long-range shooting and high work rate.

data.name: Mohammad Abualnadi
data.description: Born just outside Kansas City, Abualnadi played youth football in the US before being picked up by the Jordan football association. “Having been with Sporting Kansas City academy, trained with the first team and played with the second I took a bit of a different route to others as I ended up going to university,” he told the #TAGpodcast. “University was super important to me. I got into Notre Dame and then I transferred to Pittsburgh, top universities and very difficult to get into.” With both his parents being from Jordan, he speaks Arabic, and went on to represent the Olympic team and then the senior national team.  At club level, he featured for Al-Hussein before moving abroad to Malaysia, where he now plays for Selangor. Stands out for his composure and confidence despite limited international experience while his calm presence at the back and ability to read the game have quickly earned him recognition within the national team setup.

data.name: Salim Obaid
data.description: An experienced central defender who has earned 10 caps at the time of writing, though he continues to fight to retain his place after disappointing performances in the Arab Cup. He has played for several Jordanian clubs, including Al-Ahli, Al-Wehdat, Shabab Al-Ordon and Sahab, before settling at Al-Hussein, where he won the Jordanian Pro League title last season. Started as a regular with the national team at the beginning of the latest Arab Cup campaign, but lost his place as the tournament progressed.

data.name: Mohammad Taha
data._verification: replaced injured Ibrahim Sabra on the final squad list; Wikipedia's own preliminary-squad citation names a different player (Mohammad Abu Ghoush) for this slot, but the structured official squad table and an independent match-day lineup (Jordan v Argentina, 27 Jun 2026) both confirm Mohammad Taha, shirt 18

data.name: Saed Al-Rosan
data.description: The captain of Al-Hussein, he has led the club to back-to-back Jordanian Pro League titles. His rise to prominence was cemented by his key role in Jordan’s run to the 2025 Arab Cup finals. He began his career with Al-Arabi before moving through Mansheyat, Al-Baqa’a and Ma’an, eventually joining Al-Hussein in 2021, where he found stability and success. Made his mark internationally by scoring in Jordan’s 3-1 group stage win over Kuwait in the Arab Cup. Known for his calm personality and work rate, he is valued for his leadership on and off the pitch.

data.name: Mohannad Abu Taha
data.description: His reliability and tactical discipline have made him an essential option on the left flank, despite being naturally right-footed. Possesses a powerful strike which he showcased with a superb goal from a free-kick against Kuwait in the group stage of the 2025 Arab Cup. His performances have placed him among the players tipped for a future move to Europe. A product of Al-Wehdat’s academy, he also played for Al-Karkh in Iraq and Al-Orobah in the Saudi Pro League before settling at Al-Quwa Al-Jawiya. Came close to joining the Belgian side Gent in 2023, but the transfer collapsed due to delayed paperwork.

data.name: Nizar Al-Rashdan
data.description: A key figure in Jordan’s midfield, combining strong defensive duties with the ability to push forward and threaten from long range. A graduate of Al-Hussein, he later moved to Al-Faisaly before building a diverse career abroad. He featured for Newroz in Iraq and Emirates Club in the UAE, where he played alongside Andrés Iniesta, in addition to spells with Al-Khaldiya in Bahrain and Al-Zawraa in Iraq. Asked about playing with the Spanish maestro, he told Kooora: “I am living a beautiful experience and it has been made more beautiful by my presence alongside international stars such as Andrés Iniesta and Paco Alcácer.” Al-Rashdan now plays in the Qatari league with Qatar SC. For Jordan, al-Rashdan has scored four goals at the time of writing, most notably a dramatic stoppage-time winner against Iraq at the 2023 AFC Asian Cup.

data.name: Abdallah Al-Fakhouri
data.description: Al-Fakhouri has worked his way back into the plans of Jordan's head coach, Jamal Sellami, after a difficult period marked by a dip in form and his selection for the final squad for the World Cup reflected this renewed confidence in his abilities. Made his international debut at 18, but has since been limited to 16 caps at the time of writing, largely due to Yazeed Abulaila’s dominance. He faced criticism after conceding a costly goal against Kuwait in the qualifiers, yet continues to stand out for his agility and shot-stopping ability. Maintaining focus and composure in key moments will be crucial on the international stage.

data.name: Ihsan Haddad
data.description: The captain and one of Jordan football’s most respected leaders. He recently returned to training after a serious injury that kept him sidelined for a long period. An international since 2015, Haddad has represented all four of Jordan’s biggest clubs – Al-Faisaly, Al-Wehdat, Al-Ramtha and Al-Hussein – before two successful spells in Iraq with Al-Quwa Al-Jawiya and Al-Shorta. His experience across different leagues has shaped him into a complete and disciplined full-back who delivered standout performances as captain during the 2023 AFC Asian Cup. Haddad’s influence extends beyond matchdays to the extent that the Jordan FA included him in training camps even while he was still recovering from injury.

data.name: Ali Azaizeh
data.description: The Germany-born young forward rose to prominence during the AFC Under-23 Asian Cup earlier this year. After receiving approval from the Kuwaiti side Kazma to participate, he scored four goals, attracting attention despite Jordan’s quarter-final exit to Japan on penalties. That breakthrough earned him interest from several regional clubs, leading to a loan move to Al-Shabab in the Saudi Pro League. He began his career with Al-Ramtha, contributing to their league title in 2021. Later enjoyed a successful spell with Al-Khaldiya in Bahrain, winning the Khalid bin Hamad Cup in 2025 and scoring the decisive goal in the final against Al-Riffa. Combines sharp finishing with confidence, marking him as a key talent for Jordan’s future.

data.name: Mohammad Al-Dawoud
data.description: Revived his international career after joining Al-Wehdat midway through the 2025-26 season, where his strong performances in the Jordanian Pro League earned him a return to the national team. Scored his first goal for Jordan in the 2-2 draw against Costa Rica in March, marking a remarkable comeback. The son of the former Jordan international Rateb al-Dawoud, he has played for several domestic clubs plus a spell in Bahrain with Al-Hidd before settling at Al-Wehdat.

data.name: Anas Badawi
data.description: Badawi earned his surprise call-up to the World Cup squad after injuries to Adham al-Quraishi and Issam al-Samiri, as well as the unexpected exclusion of Ahmad Assaf. He is expected to serve as the backup right-back behind the captain, Ehsan Haddad. Badawi earned his first cap in May’s 4-1 friendly defeat by Switzerland. He began his career with Al-Jazeera before joining Al-Faisaly last summer, but an injury shortly after his move sidelined him for a period of time.
